title: Feature-Market Fit: Elementary and Secondary Schools (NAICS 611110)

sections.title: Context
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Market | Elementary and Secondary Schools |
| NAICS | 611110 |
| Product Job | Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor and secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use during occupied school hours while ensuring instant single-motion evacuation capability per IBC / NFPA 101 and generating auditable compliance documentation |
| Technology class | Emergency Exit Security Devices with Remote Monitoring |
| Commodity FP | Prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability and alerting to door activation via audible alarm |
| Constraints in play | C3 (wireless range), C4 (battery life), C5 (surface mount tamper), C6 (protocol lock-in) |

Top underserved needs (OS ≥ 14): E08 (16), E06 (15), E13 (15), E14 (15), E17 (15), E20 (15), E19 (14), E23 (14)

---

sections.title: Step 09 — Technology-Level Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
### 09a — Kano Classification + ODI Scoring

| # | Feature | Kano | Time | Cost | Safety | Reliability | Skill | Stress | Pain | Confidence | Delight | **Overall** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Mechanical Alarm Trigger | must_be | 8 | 5 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 3 | **6.78** |
| F2 | Key Override | must_be | 5 | 3 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 5 | 2 | **5.22** |
| F3 | IoT Connectivity | one_dimensional | 8 | 6 | 7 | 7 | 5 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 6 | **6.78** |
| F4 | Remote Monitoring Dashboard | attractive | 8 | 7 | 7 | 6 | 6 | 8 | 8 | 8 | 8 | **7.33** |
| F5 | Predictive Maintenance | attractive | 7 | 7 | 6 | 9 | 5 | 7 | 7 | 8 | 6 | **6.89** |
| F6 | Regulatory Compliance Documentation | attractive | 9 | 8 | 8 | 7 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 9 | 7 | **8.00** |
| F7 | Retrofit Capability | one_dimensional | 9 | 9 | 3 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 8 | 5 | 6 | **6.78** |

`overall_fit = (Time + Cost + Safety + Reliability + Skill + Stress + Pain + Confidence + Delight) / 9`

### 09b — Rationale per Feature

| Feature | Kano rationale | Primary ODI drivers | Market evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1 Mechanical Alarm Trigger** | `must_be`: Every exit alarm solution in this category must produce an audible alarm on activation — its absence constitutes non-compliance with fire codes; presence is taken for granted by every buyer. 95 dB is expected table-stakes, not a differentiator. | Safety (9) — 95 dB alarm is the primary breach notification mechanism; directly addresses E08 OS=16 (detection time). Confidence (8) — mechanical trigger cannot fail silently under power loss, eliminating E06 (undetected device failures). | E08 OS=16: "Minimize time elapsed between unauthorized exit activation and first staff notification." E06 OS=15: "Minimize device failures undetected until an alarm event occurs." |
| **F2 Key Override** | `must_be`: Mandatory for operational use — custodians, PE teachers, and delivery staff must access exits daily without triggering alarms. Absence would create unacceptable false alarm frequency and disqualify deployment. | Skill (7) + Stress (7): reduces the operational burden of authorized daily exit events. Pain (7) — eliminates alarm disruption during PE class transitions, deliveries, and after-school activities. | P1 Maintenance and Use scenarios: authorized bypass is required multiple times per day in any occupied school; false alarms desensitize staff (cry-wolf risk). |
| **F3 IoT Connectivity** | `one_dimensional`: Schools choosing wireless IoT over mechanical-only (30% market share) specifically for remote event capture. More reliable wireless transmission scales proportionally to security coordinator value — each additional door covered via wireless eliminates one manual patrol route. | Pain (8) — transforms security coordinator from door-patroller to dashboard monitor (E08 OS=16). Time (8) — remote alert eliminates discovery-by-physical-inspection delay. Confidence (7) — every activation event wirelessly logged prevents silent gaps (E13 OS=15). | E08 OS=16: wireless transmission is the mechanism enabling sub-second staff notification. E13 OS=15: wireless device-status reporting enables battery health monitoring. |
| **F4 Remote Monitoring Dashboard** | `attractive`: Schools currently use mechanical-only alarms or wired panels — a portfolio dashboard is genuinely unexpected in this procurement category. Absence does not prevent alarm function, but absence means multi-building campuses rely on radio calls and walking checks. | Stress (8) — coordinator has real-time certainty about all exit states; directly addresses E24 OS=13 (uncertainty about exits being secured). Pain (8) — eliminates campus walk-around; E15 OS=13 (identify failing unit across campus). Delight (8) — district administrators discover they can view 50+ doors from a single tablet screen post-Uvalde. | E10 OS=13: event-type distinction (propped vs. alarm vs. override). E15 OS=13: multi-building failure identification. E23 OS=14 (P4 image): dashboard provides evidence of secured exits before any external party discovers a breach. |
| **F5 Predictive Maintenance** | `attractive`: Battery depletion is the dominant failure mode for wireless IoT in schools (C4 — gym exits, cafeteria doors: 5–15 activations/day). Most alternatives do not proactively report device health. Absence creates reactive failure discovery after monitoring is already lost. | Reliability (9) — directly addresses E13 OS=15 and E14 OS=15, the joint 2nd-highest underserved needs: both concern battery depletion coverage gaps. Confidence (8) — coordinator knows no door is monitoring-dark due to unannounced battery failure. | E13 OS=15: "Minimize monitoring gaps caused by undetected battery depletion." E14 OS=15: "Increase ability to receive advance warning of battery degradation at individual door units." |
| **F6 Regulatory Compliance Documentation** | `attractive`: Post-Uvalde legislation in 32+ US states has made auditable exit monitoring logs an explicit purchasing driver — not a nice-to-have. Automated documentation directly addresses the highest-value need cluster in the CONCLUDE and MODIFY steps. | Pain (9) + Time (9) — eliminates manual log assembly labor before state inspections (E19 OS=14). Confidence (9) — E20 OS=15: continuous documentation eliminates compliance audit findings. Safety (8) — E17 OS=15: alarm silencing events are automatically logged; no gap in audit trail. | E17 OS=15: "Minimize likelihood of alarm-silencing without audit log entry." E19 OS=14: "Minimize labor to compile door event logs into audit format." E20 OS=15: "Minimize compliance audit findings from monitoring documentation gaps." E22 OS=13 (P3): "Present auditable compliance records demonstrating post-Uvalde requirements continuously met." |
| **F7 Retrofit Capability** | `one_dimensional`: All 49-year-average-age buildings in this market either lack conduit to secondary exits or face cost-prohibitive electrical retrofit. More retrofit-friendly = proportionally lower total project cost. Direct competitive axis vs. wired panel (55% market share at $150–250/hr electrician labor). | Cost (9) + Time (9) — eliminates electrician labor entirely; 50-door school deployment saves ~$30,000–50,000 vs. wired; E02 OS=13 is the primary cost barrier. Skill (9) — facilities crew install without electrical license; addresses E02 directly. Pain (8) — removes the single most painful barrier for budget-constrained aging-stock districts. | E02 OS=13: "Minimize total electrician labor hours required to install exit alarm monitoring systems." E03: "Minimize likelihood installation requires structural modification to door frame." |

### 09c — Feature-Need Coverage Map

| Need | OS | F1 | F2 | F3 | F4 | F5 | F6 | F7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E08 — detection time: unauthorized activation → staff alert | 16 | ● | | ◐ | ◐ | | | |
| E06 — undetected device failures before school day | 15 | ● | | | ◐ | ◐ | | |
| E13 — monitoring gaps from undetected battery depletion | 15 | | | ◐ | | ● | | |
| E14 — advance battery degradation warning | 15 | | | ◐ | | ● | | |
| E17 — alarm silenced without audit log entry | 15 | | | | | | ● | |
| E20 — compliance audit findings from documentation gaps | 15 | | | | | | ● | |
| E19 — labor to compile door event logs for audits | 14 | | | | | | ● | |
| E23 — unsecured exit visible to outsiders before staff notified | 14 | | | ◐ | ● | | | |
| E02 — electrician labor hours for installation | 13 | | | | | | | ● |
| E05 — time to verify all units operational each morning | 13 | | | | ◐ | ● | | |
| E10 — distinguish event types on monitoring dashboard | 13 | | | ◐ | ● | | | |
| E15 — identify failing unit across multi-building campus | 13 | | | ◐ | ● | | | |
| E22 — present auditable post-Uvalde compliance records | 13 | | | | | | ● | |
| E24 — uncertainty about all exits being secured | 13 | | | ◐ | ● | | | |

● = primary contributor | ◐ = partial contributor

---

sections.title: Step 10 — Product-Related Needs (Burleson Product Jobs)
sections.content:
Needs that arise FROM this mechanism — would disappear if a wired panel, LoRaWAN native device, or flush-integrated IP69K-rated system were chosen.

| ID | Statement | Source Constraint | Affected Markets | Importance | Satisfaction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PR1 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless monitoring coverage gaps at emergency exit doors in reinforced concrete school buildings where 868 MHz EasyWave signal attenuation reduces transmission reliability below the 30 m single-hop threshold, requiring repeater chain infrastructure that increases per-door deployment cost | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | Elementary and Secondary Schools, General Warehousing, Hospitals | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| PR2 | Minimize the likelihood of undetected alarm monitoring failure at high-traffic emergency exit doors — gymnasium exits, cafeteria doors, sports facility perimeter exits — where daily activation frequency (5–15 events/day) exhausts a 9V block battery before the 2-year scheduled maintenance target | IoT Battery Life Floor (C4) | Elementary and Secondary Schools, Hospitals, Facilities Support Services | 8.0 | 3.0 |
| PR3 | Minimize the time required to schedule and execute battery replacement across all wireless exit alarm units distributed across a multi-building school campus before individual unit monitoring capability is interrupted — a recurring maintenance burden absent in wired alternatives | IoT Battery Life Floor (C4) | Elementary and Secondary Schools, Hotels, Facilities Support Services | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| PR4 | Minimize the integration effort required to relay EasyWave alarm events into district-standard access control or building management platforms standardized on BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols — caused by the absence of native open-protocol output on the 868 MHz transmitter, requiring a GfS-proprietary BUS gateway intermediary | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In (C6) | Elementary and Secondary Schools, Hotels, Hospitals, Smart Office Buildings | 7.0 | 3.0 |
| PR5 | Minimize the likelihood of deliberate removal or physical displacement of surface-mounted exit alarm housing units by unsupervised student populations in middle and high school corridors where the external mounting profile is accessible at door-handle height without tools | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount (C5) | Elementary and Secondary Schools | 7.0 | 4.0 |

**Litmus test applied:**
- PR1: A wired panel or LoRaWAN device has different propagation physics → disappears ✓
- PR2: A wired/mains-powered or large-battery device has no 9V depletion problem → disappears ✓
- PR3: A wired device requires no battery scheduling → disappears ✓
- PR4: A BACnet-native or open-protocol device would integrate without gateway → disappears ✓
- PR5: A flush-integrated or tamper-proof door hardware solution is not physically accessible → disappears ✓

---

Confidence (7): every activation event wirelessly logged prevents silent coverage gaps (E13 OS=15).

attractive: portfolio-level real-time visibility is genuinely unexpected in this procurement category — schools currently rely on radio calls and physical patrol. Stress (8): real-time certainty about all exits addresses E24 OS=13. Pain (8): eliminates multi-building walk-around; E15 OS=13 (identify failing unit). Delight (8): district administrators who have used clipboards discover they can view all 50+ doors from a tablet — high delight especially post-Uvalde.

attractive: battery depletion is the dominant IoT failure mode in schools (gym exits: 5–15 activations/day; C4 dominant). Proactive alerts are not expected from any alternative in the market. Reliability (9): directly addresses E13 OS=15 and E14 OS=15 — joint 2nd highest underserved needs; both are battery coverage gap needs. Confidence (8): coordinator knows no door is monitoring-dark before the next maintenance visit.

attractive: post-Uvalde legislation in 32+ US states makes auditable exit monitoring logs an explicit purchasing driver. Automated documentation directly addresses the highest-value underserved need cluster. Pain (9) + Time (9): eliminates manual log assembly before state inspections (E19 OS=14). Confidence (9): E20 OS=15 — continuous records eliminate compliance audit findings. Safety (8): E17 OS=15 — alarm-silencing actions auto-logged; no audit trail gap. Highest overall_fit of all features: 8.00.

one_dimensional: all 49-year-average-age school buildings lack conduit to secondary exits; more retrofit-friendly proportionally reduces total project cost. Cost (9): eliminates electrician labor ($150–250/hr); 50-door school saves ~$30,000–50,000 vs. wired; E02 OS=13. Skill (9): facilities crew install without electrical license. Safety (3): installation ease does not affect post-deployment safety outcomes.

entities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of wireless monitoring coverage gaps at emergency exit doors in reinforced concrete school buildings where 868 MHz EasyWave signal attenuation reduces transmission reliability below the 30 m single-hop threshold — requiring repeater chain infrastructure that increases per-door deployment cost

Minimize the likelihood of undetected alarm monitoring failure at high-traffic emergency exit doors — gymnasium exits, cafeteria doors, sports facility perimeter exits — where daily activation frequency (5–15 events/day) exhausts a 9V block battery before the 2-year scheduled maintenance target

Minimize the time required to schedule and execute battery replacement across all wireless exit alarm units distributed across a multi-building school campus before individual unit monitoring capability is interrupted

Minimize the integration effort required to relay EasyWave alarm events into district-standard access control or building management platforms standardized on BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols — caused by the absence of native open-protocol output on the 868 MHz transmitter, requiring a GfS-proprietary BUS gateway intermediary

Minimize the likelihood of deliberate removal or physical displacement of surface-mounted exit alarm housing units by unsupervised student populations in middle and high school corridors where the external mounting profile is accessible at door-handle height without tools
